We've spent a good amount of time talking about the hostage negotiation judo that the Black Swan group has made the stock of our trade. The calibrated questions, the mirrors, the labels, etc. At the end of the day, though, your negotiations sometimes come down to determining who gets which slice at a pie. From time to time, you're going to encounter bare-knuckle bargaining. As you might imagine, when Chris was in the FBI, he was always on the lookout for new techniques and methods. One that he learned and we still swear by to this day, we call the Ackerman Model. It's based on a system developed by renowned XCIA agent Mike Ackerman. On the surface, the Ackerman Model looks like an offer-counteroffer sequential move bargaining strategy. But it beats, hands down, the typical offer-counteroffer meet-in-the-middle bargaining nonsense. It was designed to get the best deal possible with the toughest bare-knuckle bargainers in the world, kidnappers. The Ackerman Model is a very simple and easy-to-remember process. Number one, set your target price or your goal. Number two, calculate your first offer at 65% of your target price. Number three, calculate three raises of decreasing increments at roughly 20%, 10%, and 5% of your target. Number four, here is the Black Swan Group add-in. Use relentless tactical empathy and Black Swan ways of saying no to get the other side to counter before you increase your offer. Work like hell to get them to bid against themselves. Number five, when calculating the final amount, use precise, non-rounded numbers like $37,893 rather than $37,500, as this adds credibility and weight to the number. Number six, on your final number, throw in a non-monetary item that they probably don't want to show you're at your limit. As an example, in one negotiation in the food service industry, they offered a year's supply of smoothies every time their counterpart might be in town and could come into their store. The genius of this system is that it integrates all the psychological tactics that we've discussed up to now. So you can better understand this game-changing strategy. Let's explore each step a little more in depth. For the sake of this example, let's say your target goal is $1,000. Number one, your first offer is $650. Note this doesn't mean you go first. This is your number regardless of what the other side says. If they go first and go high, fine. Ignorant. In cases where they actually come in below where you want to end up, you've got to shift gears into, how am I supposed to do that? As a way of buying yourself the time to not jump the gun. If you jump the gun, you will leave them disappointed when they realize it was too easy. Too easy equals implementation problems, if not all out reneg on the deal. The first offer of 65% of your target price will serve as an anchor, which has the potential to bring your counterpart right to their price limit. Sheared disbelief triggered by an extreme anchor will induce the fight or flight reaction in all but the most experienced negotiators. This will limit their thinking and tend to push them towards making a rash decision. Since you don't want to induce a rash decision, its critical tactical empathy comes first. You might say, you're going to be disappointed by this. Before you throw your number, then go silent. Let their own defense mechanisms kick in. Count 1000s to 10. Whatever they imagine, it will be worse than the number you throw, which plays to your advantage in eliminating their flight response. Your number may actually be a relief. Before you get to 10 on your count, they will say something that lets you know it's okay to give them the number. If you get to 10, label the silence. Sounds like you don't want to hear it. Give the number when you've got permission to do so. The secret to gaining the upper hand in a negotiation is giving the other side the illusion of control. The following three steps are a sequence of progressively increasing the offer to 800, 900, and 950 after each of the other side's counter offers. Note, these increases are based on 20%, 10%, and 5% of your target number. Then finally, $987. Just below a thousand in this example. Here are the key points to this progression. Number one, this is a sequential move game, which means you don't make any more price moves until after they do. Number two, lay the tactical empathy on thick between each move. Wow, that was a very generous offer. If you don't feel like you're laying it on thick, you're not laying it on thick enough. Use plenty of versions of no, how am I supposed to do that? Or other forms. Summarize to trigger that's rights. You're working to get them to bargain against themselves if you can prompt it. Be prepared for them to cave in at any moment. If they do hit them with empathy, paraphrases, and summaries to seal it. At the very least, they will feel each of your counter moves were hard one and they will value each and every inch they feel they've gained. Number three, regardless of your actual choices of numbers, plan for three separate sets of moves. And each move has to be made in decreasing amounts. They will then feel like they're squeezing every last dime out of you. The key for them here is not did they actually make the best deal they could have, but did they feel like they made the best deal they could have? This is a critical emotional intelligence distinction, which will build the durability of your deal. This system works and it inspires your counterpart to engage and reciprocate. Just like people are more likely to send Christmas cards to people who send cards to them, they are more likely to make bargaining concessions to those who have made concessions. Secondly, the diminishing size of the increasing persuades your counterpart they are winning. By the time you get to your last offer, your counterpart feels like they've really gotten every last drop out of you and it raises their self-esteem. Research has shown that people getting concessions often feel better about the bargaining process than those who received and accept one firm, fair offer. This is true even when they end up paying more or getting less in return. When you finally get close to 100% of your asking price, make sure you hit them with an odd, non-round number. As we have mentioned time and time again, this gives your counterpart the impression that you carefully calculated exactly how much you could afford. It makes your number more immovable. If you have to take it a step further, throw in some non-monetary item that they likely don't want. This convinces your counterpart that you are truly at your maximum and cannot go any further. This system also works if you need to flip it and are the seller. Apply all the same rules. One caveat, the more personal the commodity to the person on the other side, such as the sale of a home or business they founded, the less tolerance they will possess to the rounds of bargaining. Therefore, the tactical empathy becomes more critical. The Ackerman model encompasses the best parts of all the techniques that we have covered in this course. Preparation is key. When the pressure is on, you don't rise to the occasion. You fall to your highest level of preparation. Understanding your counterpart's type will help you hone your approach and keep you from misunderstanding their signals based on tight mismatch. It will give you insights during times of negotiation impasse. They are not you. Design an ambitious goal and then plan out the labels, calibrated questions, and responses you'll use to get there. That way, once you're at the bargaining table, you'll be prepared. When you're planning out your strategy, you've got to ensure that you can take a punch. Many experienced negotiators will hit you with an extreme anchor to try to knock you off your game. If you're not prepared, you may well cave in and go straight to your maximum. However, if you've envisioned yourself reacting calmly, you will react calmly. You'll be prepared and won't get sucked into the compromise trap. Tactical empathy allows you to regain the initiative and to set boundaries. When you lose your temper, the game is over. And finally, always keep in mind that the person across the table is not the adversary. The situation is.
